name: Houji Chinese - Grain God
shortDescription: Houji is a Chinese deity associated with agriculture and grains, particularly rice. He is revered as a patron of farmers and a provider of sustenance.
description: Houji, also known as Lord Millet, is a significant figure in Chinese mythology and is revered as the god of agriculture, specifically associated with millet and grains. While primarily a Chinese deity, his influence extends into Japanese beliefs due to cultural exchange. Houji is credited with teaching humanity the techniques of agriculture, including sowing seeds, cultivating crops, and harvesting grains. He is often depicted as a wise and benevolent figure, dedicated to ensuring plentiful harvests and the well-being of the people. Legends surrounding Houji's birth vary, but many involve miraculous or divine origins, emphasizing his importance to agriculture. He is regarded as a culture hero and a symbol of prosperity, ensuring the continuation of food supply. His association with rice connects him indirectly to deities like Inari in Japan, as Inari is also associated with rice, fertility, and prosperity. The importance of Houji highlights the profound significance of agriculture in ancient Chinese society and its enduring influence on subsequent cultures, including Japan. While his direct worship in Japan is less prominent compared to China, the underlying principles of his role – ensuring bountiful harvests and the well-being of farmers – resonate with the broader agricultural themes present in Japanese Shinto and folk beliefs.

sources.title: Records of the Grand Historian (Shi Ji)
sources.author: Sima Qian
